summary: This lesson teaches that verbs in Spanish often take different prepositions than their English equivalents, and encourages analytical curiosity about why. Key examples: enamorarse de (to fall in love with), pensar en (to think about), soñar con (to dream about), depender de (to depend on). Also introduces conmigo/contigo and sí as 'if'.

teachings.summary: Spanish verbs often use different prepositions than their English equivalents. Rather than memorizing blindly, analyzing why the preposition differs can aid memory and reveal how language shapes perception. Key pairings: enamorarse DE (fall in love WITH), pensar EN (think ABOUT), soñar CON (dream ABOUT), depender DE (depend ON).

examples.english: I fall in love with you.
I fall in love with him.
It depends on if she wants to go.

teachings.notes: Enamorarse de: 'of' is more detached than 'with', possibly reflecting different cultural perceptions of love. Soñar con: 'with' implies the dreamed person participates, possibly an old belief. Pensar en: may come from Arabic influence during 800 years of contact.

teachings.summary: To fall in love is 'enamorarse de' - literally 'to enamor oneself of'. It is a reflexive verb found in the dictionary as enamorarse. Uses the preposition de, not con.
teachings.rule: Enamorarse de = to fall in love with. Me enamoro de = I fall in love with.

examples.english: I fall in love with you.
I am falling in love with him.

teachings.summary: 'With me' and 'with you (informal)' have special forms: conmigo and contigo, written as single words. This is an exception to the normal preposition + pronoun pattern (de mí, para ti are two words, but conmigo/contigo are one).
teachings.rule: con + mí = conmigo (with me). con + ti = contigo (with you informal). Written as one word.

examples.english: Do you want to come with me?
Why don't you want to come with me?
You have to come with me.

teachings.notes: The -go in conmigo comes from nowhere historically. All other prepositions use mí/ti as separate words.

teachings.summary: The word for 'yes' (sí) is also the word for 'if' in Spanish.

examples.english: It depends on if she wants to go.

teachings.summary: Soñar means to dream. The o splits to ue when stressed (sueño, sueña, sueñan, sueñas) but not when unstressed (soñamos). The ñ is the only letter in Spanish not in English, pronounced like ny.
teachings.rule: Soñar + con = to dream about. O splits to ue when stressed: sueño, sueña. Does not split in soñamos.
teachings.notes: The ñ (n with tilde) is pronounced ny, as in mañana. There is no way to predict which verbs will have the o-to-ue split; if it happens in one stressed form, it happens in all stressed forms.

teachings.summary: To depend is depender, an -er verb. In Spanish you say 'depende de' (depends of) rather than 'depends on'.
teachings.rule: depender de = to depend on. Uses de, not en or another preposition.

examples.english: It depends on if she wants to go.

teachings.notes: Common mistake: conjugating depender as an -ar verb. Always check the verb type first.

vocabulary.derivation: en + amor + arse = to enamor oneself
